procedure_overview: Plantar Fasciitis Release (decompression_or_release). Certified. Score 4. Source-backed from v3.
must_know_anatomy: Plantar fascia: thick aponeurosis with central (strongest, to 2-4 toes), medial (to hallux), and lateral bands. Central band most commonly involved in fasciitis.
Release: open or endoscopic. Incision medial or plantar. Release only medial 1/3 to 2/3 of central band to avoid lateral column instability and arch collapse.
Baxter nerve (first branch of lateral plantar nerve): at risk in medial/plantar approaches; compression or iatrogenic injury causes heel pain (Baxter neuritis) mimicking or coexisting with fasciitis.
Medial calcaneal nerve: sensory to heel; at risk in medial incisions. Injury causes painful heel numbness or neuroma.
Plantar fat pad: important for shock absorption under calcaneus; preserve during plantar approaches.
Medial/lateral plantar vessels/nerves: deeper; at risk with over-aggressive or misdirected release. Over-release leads to flatfoot or lateral column pain.
surgical_approach_anatomy: Medial or plantar incision (open) or endoscopic portals. For open: medial heel incision, identify central band, release medial 1/3-2/3 under direct vision while protecting fat pad and nerves. Endoscopic: portals for visualization and release with care for Baxter.
Key protection: identify and protect Baxter nerve (1st lat plantar branch) and medial calcaneal nerve. Limit release to avoid lateral band and arch destabilization. Preserve fat pad.
Post-release: confirm adequate but not excessive release. Close carefully. Post-op protected weightbearing, stretching, and monitor for lateral column or nerve issues.

structures_at_risk.structure: Baxter nerve (1st branch of lateral plantar nerve)
structures_at_risk.why_at_risk: Runs between abductor hallucis and quadratus plantae near medial calcaneal tuberosity; compressed in fasciitis or injured during medial/plantar release. Common cause of recalcitrant heel pain.
structures_at_risk.how_to_avoid_injury: Identify during medial approach (between abductor hallucis and quadratus). Protect with retractor during release. Endoscopic requires careful portal placement and visualization.
structures_at_risk.consequence_of_injury: Heel pain (Baxter neuritis), abductor hallucis weakness, or painful neuroma. Can be more symptomatic than original fasciitis.
structures_at_risk.approach_context: Medial or endoscopic plantar fascia release

structures_at_risk.why_at_risk: Sensory branch to medial heel skin; at risk in medial heel incisions for open release.
structures_at_risk.how_to_avoid_injury: Identify and protect under skin in medial approach. Avoid blind dissection or cautery. Use careful incision placement.
structures_at_risk.consequence_of_injury: Medial heel numbness or painful neuroma. Can cause chronic heel pain.

structures_at_risk.structure: Lateral plantar nerve / vessels and fat pad
structures_at_risk.why_at_risk: Deeper structures; over-release or misdirection can injure lateral plantar or destabilize lateral column via fat pad or band disruption.
structures_at_risk.how_to_avoid_injury: Limit release to medial 1/3-2/3 central band. Preserve fat pad and lateral band. Direct visualization for open; careful endoscopic technique.
structures_at_risk.consequence_of_injury: Lateral column pain, flatfoot deformity, or vascular/nerve injury to lateral foot. Arch collapse or transfer metatarsalgia.

structures_at_risk.why_at_risk: Shock-absorbing pad; thinned or damaged in plantar approaches, leading to painful heel or altered biomechanics.
structures_at_risk.how_to_avoid_injury: Preserve fat pad during dissection and release. Avoid excessive plantar exposure or retraction that thins it.
structures_at_risk.consequence_of_injury: Painful heel (fat pad atrophy), altered gait, or need for orthotics/padding.

key_landmarks: Essential palpable landmark from approach or reduction content.

Essential palpable landmark from approach or reduction content.
common_mistakes: Over-release (full or lateral band) leading to lateral column pain, flatfoot, or arch collapse.
Missing or injuring Baxter nerve (1st lat plantar branch), causing persistent or new heel neuritis (often worse than fasciitis).
Injuring medial calcaneal nerve in medial incision (heel numbness or neuroma).
Damaging or thinning plantar fat pad (painful heel atrophy, need for orthotics).
Failing to limit release or monitor post-op for transfer problems or missed nerve compression.
attending_pimp_questions: Q: What portion of the plantar fascia is typically released and why limit it? A: Medial 1/3 to 2/3 of the central band only. Full or lateral release destabilizes the lateral column and arch, causing pain, flatfoot, or transfer lesions.

Q: Which nerve is commonly involved in recalcitrant heel pain (Baxter neuritis) and how is it protected during release? A: First branch of lateral plantar (Baxter) nerve, between abductor hallucis and quadratus plantae. Identify and protect during medial approach; injury or compression causes heel pain mimicking fasciitis.

Q: What sensory nerve is at risk in medial heel incision for open plantar release and consequence? A: Medial calcaneal nerve (sensory to heel skin). Injury causes numbness or painful neuroma on medial heel. Identify under skin and protect.

Q: Why preserve the plantar fat pad during release and what happens if damaged? A: It is the main shock absorber under the calcaneus. Damage causes painful heel atrophy, altered gait, and need for padding/orthotics.

Q: What are the risks of over-release or missing Baxter nerve in plantar fasciitis surgery? A: Lateral column pain, arch collapse, flatfoot, or persistent/new neuritis pain (often worse than original). Limited release + nerve protection + post-op care are essential.
night_before_review_checklist: Review linked modules and source URLs
Confirm any case-specific fluoroscopy or approach details
limitations: Anatomy-focused case prep only; does not replace full surgical technique or attending-specific preferences
Always verify with latest imaging and team briefing

surgical_layers.layer_name: Superficial (skin to plantar fascia / abductor hallucis)
surgical_layers.what_user_should_know: Medial heel incision (open) or endoscopic portals. Identify central band of plantar fascia. Protect medial calcaneal nerve under skin and Baxter nerve (between abductor hallucis and quadratus plantae).
key_structures: Central plantar fascia band (medial 1/3-2/3 for release)
surgical_layers.surgical_relevance: Standard approach for release. Nerve identification (Baxter especially) and limited release (medial 1/3-2/3) prevent complications like neuritis or lateral column collapse.

surgical_layers.layer_name: Deep (fascia release and protection of deeper structures)
surgical_layers.what_user_should_know: Release only medial 1/3 to 2/3 of central band under direct vision (open) or with endoscopic control. Preserve lateral band and fat pad. Protect lateral plantar nerve/vessels deeper. Confirm adequate but not excessive release.
surgical_layers.surgical_relevance: The release layer. Limited, protected release is key to success; over-release or nerve injury causes worse problems than original fasciitis.

surgical_layers.what_user_should_know: Close in layers if open. Protected weightbearing, stretching, night splint, and orthotics post-op. Monitor for lateral column pain, nerve symptoms, or arch collapse. Most improve with time; revision rare but for over-release or missed Baxter.
surgical_layers.surgical_relevance: Closure and aftercare determine outcome. Limited release + protection + monitoring prevent transfer problems or missed neuritis.

validation_warnings: Plantar fasciitis release payload is medial/plantar approach focused with emphasis on nerve protection (Baxter) and limited release. Endoscopic vs open technique varies; this covers key anatomy. Source material previously mixed ankle/Achilles/Lisfranc and contained stringified/empty/generic/placeholders; cleaned to public facts and rich 6-field content only.
danger_zones: Baxter nerve (1st lat plantar): identify/protect in medial approach (abductor/quadratus plane); injury causes recalcitrant heel pain (neuritis).
Medial calcaneal nerve: protect in medial incision; injury causes heel numbness/neuroma.
Lateral band / column: limit release to medial 1/3-2/3 central; over-release causes lateral pain, flatfoot, arch collapse.
Plantar fat pad: preserve for shock absorption; damage causes painful heel atrophy.
approach_specific_notes: This payload is for plantar fasciitis release (open medial or endoscopic). Focus on central band anatomy, limited medial 1/3-2/3 release, Baxter (1st lat plantar) and medial calcaneal nerve protection, fat pad preservation, and avoiding lateral column/arch destabilization. No Achilles, ankle ligament, Lisfranc, or hip content mixed. Content is plantar fascia-specific.
